topic: Why people obey authority even when they should not — Milgram

script.title: Why You Would Flip the Switch Too — Milgram

scenes.narration: In 1961, an ordinary person sat down in a lab and agreed to electrocute a stranger. Not a monster. A neighbor. A teacher. Someone exactly like you.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a heavy electrical switch mounted on a cracked wall, long shadow cast across the floor, single overhead light

scenes.narration: Stanley Milgram wanted to understand how the Nazis happened. His answer was terrifying — it was not them. It was everyone. It was obedience.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir: rows of empty chairs in a sterile room, harsh fluorescent shadows, authoritative desk at the far end, no people

scenes.narration: Subjects were told to deliver electric shocks every time someone answered wrong. The shocks were not real. The screams were recordings. But the subjects did not know that.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir: close-up of hands gripping a control panel dial, knuckles white with tension, deep shadow across the frame

scenes.narration: 65 percent of people delivered the maximum shock — 450 volts — simply because a man in a lab coat said please continue. Authority spoke. The switch was flipped.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir: a lone figure at a table, face hidden in shadow, hand resting on a lever, oppressive darkness surrounding

scenes.narration: When participants hesitated, they heard four phrases. Please continue. The experiment requires it. You have no other choice. That last one was a lie — but it worked.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir: a locked door with light bleeding under it, a key hanging just out of reach on a hook, chains on the floor

scenes.narration: Your brain has two systems fighting constantly — your conscience, and your need to comply with perceived authority. Milgram proved which one usually wins.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir: a cracked mirror reflecting two divergent shadowy paths, one lit dimly, one dark, no human forms

scenes.narration: The scariest part? Obedient subjects did not enjoy it. They sweated. They trembled. They kept going anyway. Discomfort does not equal resistance.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir: a chair bolted to the floor under a single harsh spotlight, leather straps hanging loose, ominous empty stillness

scenes.narration: Milgram did not discover weakness. He discovered design — systems built to make obedience the path of least resistance. The question is: who designed yours?
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir: a long empty hallway receding into darkness, a single open door at the far end letting in a sliver of cold light
